quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Angular position (θ)"?

options.text: A force whose work is path-independent; equivalently, the work around any closed path is zero; examples: gravity, spring force
F_avg = Δp/Δt = J/Δt; the constant force that would deliver the same impulse over the same time interval
f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N
The angle of rotation measured in radians from a reference direction; one complete revolution = 2π radians

quiz_questions.explanation: "Angular position (θ)" is defined as: The angle of rotation measured in radians from a reference direction; one complete revolution = 2π radians. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Arc length and angle"?

options.text: F = Gm₁m₂/r²; every pair of masses attracts each other with a force proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance
J = ∫F dt = Δp; the time integral of force equals the change in momentum
The contact force perpendicular to a surface, adjusting to prevent objects from passing through each other
s = rθ; the arc length swept by a point at radius r when the object rotates through angle θ (in radians)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Arc length and angle" is defined as: s = rθ; the arc length swept by a point at radius r when the object rotates through angle θ (in radians). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Rotational kinematic equations"?

options.text: In collisions, impulsive forces are so large and brief that other forces (gravity, friction) are negligible during the collision
1 hp = 746 W; a unit of power commonly used for engines and motors
½mv₁² - GMm/r₁ = ½mv₂² - GMm/r₂; used to find speed at any orbital distance without knowing forces
ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ-θ₀); for constant α

quiz_questions.explanation: "Rotational kinematic equations" is defined as: ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ-θ₀); for constant α. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Angular Kinematics, which best defines "Angular velocity (ω)"?

options.text: Friction converts kinetic energy to thermal energy; work done by friction equals -f_k × d, always negative
N = mgcosθ on a frictionless incline; modified by other forces pressing against or pulling away from the surface
F_c = mv²/r = mrω²; the net radial force required to maintain circular motion at a given radius and speed
ω = dθ/dt; measured in rad/s; related to linear velocity by v = rω

quiz_questions.explanation: In Angular Kinematics, "Angular velocity (ω)" refers to: ω = dθ/dt; measured in rad/s; related to linear velocity by v = rω.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Angular Kinematics, which best defines "Angular acceleration (α)"?

options.text: α = dω/dt; measured in rad/s²; related to tangential linear acceleration by a_t = rα
The gravitational force on an object is the vector sum of the gravitational forces from all other masses
f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N
F = -kx; the restoring force of an ideal spring is proportional to displacement from equilibrium and directed opposite to it

quiz_questions.explanation: In Angular Kinematics, "Angular acceleration (α)" refers to: α = dω/dt; measured in rad/s²; related to tangential linear acceleration by a_t = rα.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The angle of rotation measured in radians from a reference direction; one complete revolution = 2π radians"?

options.text: Vector
Power
Angular position (θ)
Velocity of center of mass

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Angular position (θ)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "s = rθ; the arc length swept by a point at radius r when the object rotates through angle θ (in radians)"?

options.text: Escape condition
Arc length and angle
Apparent weight
Geosynchronous orbit

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Arc length and angle".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ-θ₀); for constant α"?

options.text: Rotational kinematic equations
Binding energy
Impulse approximation
Simple pendulum period

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Rotational kinematic equations".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Angular Kinematics, which term matches: "ω = dθ/dt; measured in rad/s; related to linear velocity by v = rω"?

options.text: Angular velocity (ω)
Transverse vs. longitudinal
Potential energy
Angular momentum (particle)

quiz_questions.explanation: In Angular Kinematics, this refers to "Angular velocity (ω)".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Angular Kinematics, which term matches: "α = dω/dt; measured in rad/s²; related to tangential linear acceleration by a_t = rα"?

options.text: Angular acceleration (α)
Average power
Impulse-momentum theorem
Rotational kinematics

quiz_questions.explanation: In Angular Kinematics, this refers to "Angular acceleration (α)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Angular Kinematics is accurate?

options.text: Angular Kinematics describes rotational motion using angular position θ(t), angular velocity ω(t) = dθ/dt, and angular acceleration α(t) = dω/dt.
Setting gravitational force equal to centripetal force: GMm/r² = mv²/r gives orbital speed v = √(GM/r) and period T = 2π√(r³/GM), which is Kepler's Third Law.
Work is defined as W = ∫F·dr (the dot product integral of force along the displacement path), and the Work-Energy Theorem states W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv₀².
Real collisions are partially inelastic.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Angular Kinematics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Angular Kinematics?

options.text: Momentum is the product of mass and velocity (p = mv) and represents the quantity of motion of an object.
For constant angular acceleration, the rotational kinematic equations parallel their linear counterparts.
The critical condition at the top of a vertical loop (minimum speed for maintaining contact) occurs when the normal force equals zero: mg = mv²/r, giving v_min = √(gr).
Work is defined as W = ∫F·dr (the dot product integral of force along the displacement path), and the Work-Energy Theorem states W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv₀².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Angular Kinematics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Angular Kinematics?

options.text: Universal Gravitation states that every mass attracts every other mass with force F = Gm₁m₂/r², where G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².
The connection between linear and angular quantities—s = rθ, v = rω, a_t = rα—links the motion of a rotating body to the translational motion of points on it.
Conservative Forces and Potential Energy connect force to potential energy through F = -dU/dr (in one dimension) or F = -∇U (in three dimensions).
For variable forces (such as springs: F = -kx), integration is essential: W_spring = ∫(-kx)dx = -½kx².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Angular Kinematics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Angular Kinematics?

options.text: Power analysis is critical for engine design, electrical systems, and athletic performance.
The angular frequency ω = √(k/m) depends on system parameters (spring constant and mass), not amplitude.
Angular Kinematics describes rotational motion using angular position θ(t), angular velocity ω(t) = dθ/dt, and angular acceleration α(t) = dω/dt.
Energy oscillates between kinetic (½mv²) and potential (½kx²) with total energy E = ½kA² constant.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Angular Kinematics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Angular Kinematics?

options.text: Gravitational Potential Energy in the universal form is U = -GMm/r, where r is the distance between centers of mass.
This inverse-square law governs planetary orbits, satellite motion, and tidal effects.

Energy conservation with U = -GMm/r is essential for orbit analysis: total mechanical energy E = ½mv² - GMm/r determines whether an orbit is bound (E < 0) or unbound (E ≥ 0), and escape velocity follows from setting E = 0.
For constant angular acceleration, the rotational kinematic equations parallel their linear counterparts.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Angular Kinematics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Angular Kinematics?

options.text: SpaceX and NASA calculating orbital insertion burns using v = √(GM/r) to place satellites at specific altitudes and orbital velocities
CD/DVD drive technology computing read speeds at different radii using ω = v/r to maintain constant linear velocity as the read head moves
Wrench design and usage: longer handles provide greater torque for the same applied force, following τ = rF
Computer graphics and game physics engines using dot and cross products for collision detection, lighting calculations, and surface normal computations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Angular Kinematics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Angular Kinematics?

options.text: Calculating the angular deceleration of a flywheel when braking force is applied to determine stopping time and number of revolutions
Sports biomechanics analyzing the impulse delivered by a bat to a baseball using force-time graphs from high-speed sensors
GPS satellite corrections accounting for the variation of g with altitude to maintain precise timing and positioning
Analyzing asteroid capture: determining whether an approaching asteroid's total energy is negative enough to become bound to a planet's gravitational field

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Angular Kinematics.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Angular Kinematics applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Wind turbine performance analysis relating angular velocity to blade tip speed and power output at different wind conditions
Roller coaster design using potential energy diagrams to verify sufficient kinetic energy at all points along the track without external power input
Analyzing braking distance on inclined roads where both gravity component and friction determine deceleration
Calculating the power required for an elevator motor to lift a loaded car at constant speed: P = mgv

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Angular Kinematics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Angular Kinematics?

options.text: CD/DVD drive technology computing read speeds at different radii using ω = v/r to maintain constant linear velocity as the read head moves
Automotive engineering placing a vehicle's center of mass low and centrally to optimize handling, stability, and rollover resistance
Flywheel energy storage systems using high-I, high-ω wheels to store kinetic energy for regenerative braking in vehicles and power grid stabilization
Figure skating spins: pulling arms in reduces I, increasing ω to conserve angular momentum (demonstrating I's physical significance)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Angular Kinematics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Angular Kinematics in practice?

options.text: Analyzing braking distance on inclined roads where both gravity component and friction determine deceleration
Calculating the angular deceleration of a flywheel when braking force is applied to determine stopping time and number of revolutions
Helicopter tail rotor design: compensating for the angular momentum of the main rotor to prevent the helicopter body from spinning in the opposite direction
Automobile engine specifications using power curves (horsepower vs. RPM) to match engine performance to vehicle weight and desired acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Angular Kinematics.
